studentQuestionArray.content: A client complains of severe throbbing pain on the right side of the head. Which medication will be beneficial for the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Naloxone (Narcan) is an opioid antagonist which helps to alleviate the symptoms of respiratory depression due to opioid toxicity.
Option 2:The presence of severe throbbing and unilateral pain indicates that the client has migraine. Ibuprofen (Advil) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medication that helps to alleviate pain.
Option 3:Phentolamine (Regitine) is an alpha antagonist which helps to alleviate the symptoms of hypertensive crisis caused due to monoamine oxidase inhibitors.
Option 4:Acetylcysteine (Acetadote) is an antidote which helps to alleviate the symptoms of acetaminophen (Tylenol) toxicity.

Dilute 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.9% NaCl.

Dilute 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.5% dextrose solution.
Dilute 0.4 mg ampule of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.9% NaCl.
Dilute 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.9% NaCl.
Dilute 0.4 mg ampule of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.5% dextrose solution.
Dilute 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.5% dextrose solution.

studentQuestionArray.content: The primary health-care provider has prescribed naloxone (Narcan) to a client who has fentanyl (Duragesic) toxicity. The nurse finds that the client weights 38 kg. What is the best nursing intervention in this situation?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Diluting 0.4 mg ampule of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.9% NaCl will cause overdosage and lead to seizures.
Option 2:If the client weighs less than 40 Kg, the nurse should dilute 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with 10 mL of 0.9% NaCl to prevent overdosage.
Option 3:Dextrose causes hemolysis. Therefore, the nurse should not use dextrose for diluting the medication.
Option 4:Diluting 0.1 mg of naloxone (Narcan) with dextrose causes hemolysis.

studentQuestionArray.content: What is the oral dose of ibuprofen (Advil) for an 11-year-old-child who weighs 90 lb?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The oral of ibuprofen (Advil) for an 11 year-old-child who weighs 72-95 lbs is 300 mg every 6–8 hr.
Option 2:The oral of ibuprofen (Advil) for a 6-11 month-old infant who weighs 12-17 lbs is 50 mg every 6–8 hr.
Option 3:The oral of ibuprofen (Advil) for 2 to 3-year-old-child who weighs 24-35 lbs is 100 mg every 6–8 hr.
Option 4:The oral of ibuprofen (Advil) for a 6 to 8-year-old-child who weighs 48-59 lbs is 200 mg every 6–8 hr.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which analgesic medication has a central nervous system depressant action?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Aspirin is an NSAID that reduces prostaglandin synthesis and alleviates pain. It does not act centrally and does not cause depression.
Option 2:Tramadol (Ultram) is a centrally acting nonopioid medication that has depressant action.
Option 3:Indomethacin (Indocin) is an NSAID that helps to alleviate somatic pain. It does not cause central nervous system depression.
Option 4:Mefenamic acid (Ponstel) is an NSAID and does not have central nervous system depressant action.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which drug is available as a transdermal patch?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Morphine (Oramorph) is commonly administered in oral, IV, and subcutaneous routes.
Option 2:Fentanyl (Abstral) is commonly administered as a transdermal patch and it is commonly used while providing critical care.
Option 3:Methadone (Methadose) is most commonly administered orally.
Option 4:Hydromorphone (Dilaudid) is commonly administered in oral, IV, and subcutaneous routes.

Test Taking Tips: The drug that is used as a transdermal patch ensures continuous release of drug for up to 72 hours. Select the correct answer based on which of the options require such administration.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client who is on opioid therapy complains of nausea and vomiting. The primary health-care provider has instructed the nurse to premedicate the client with hydroxyzine (Atarax) to reduce vomiting. Which complication does the nurse anticipate to find in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Hydroxyzine (Atarax) is an antihistamine medication that has a mild sedative effect. Antihistamines interact with opioid and cause excessive sedation in the client.
Option 2:Antihistamines and opioids do not impair platelet functioning and do not increase the risk of bleeding.
Option 3:Hydroxyzine (Atarax) is an antihistamine, which also has anti-allergic action; but it does not increase risk of infection.
Option 4:Antihistamine medications cause vasodilatation and hypotension.

Test Taking Tips: Recollect the side effects of antihistamine medications.

optionList.content: A blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg
A blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is monitoring a client who is on opioid therapy. Which finding, if present in the client, should be reported to the primary health-care provider immediately?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:A heart rate of 70 beats/minute is a normal finding and does not need to be reported to the health-care provider.
Option 2:A blood pressure of 110/70 mm Hg indicates that the client has a risk of hypotension. However, hypotension may not be a life-threatening condition in this case.
Option 3:A body temperature of 37.1o C is a normal finding. Therefore, the nurse will not inform the primary health-care provider.
Option 4:A respiratory rate of 8 breaths/ minute indicates that the client has difficulty in breathing. Large doses of opioids may cause central nervous system depression and lead to difficulty in breathing and respiratory depression. Therefore, when the client has low respiratory rate, the nurse should immediately report it to the primary health-care provider.

studentQuestionArray.content: A nurse is caring for a client who has chronic kidney disease and is on hemodialysis. The client complains of a severe itching sensation on his back. Which medication will be beneficial?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Aspirin is an NSAID used for treating pains and fever.
Option 2:The presence of an itching sensation on the back indicates that the client has pruritus. Diphenhydramine (Benadryl) is an antihistamine drug used for treating pruritus in clients with kidney disease.
Option 3:Naloxone (Narcan) is an opioid antagonist that is used for treating respiratory depression.
Option 4:Morphine is a mµ agonist opioid that is not preferred for treating pruritus.

studentQuestionArray.content: The nurse is caring for a client receiving acetaminophen. Which health problem would cause the nurse to be concerned related to safe administration of this drug?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Acetaminophen is a non-opioid analgesic, which causes severe hepatotoxicity in clients who has liver disease.
Option 2:Non-opioid analgesic acetaminophen does not cause any complications in clients with myocardial infarction.
Option 3:Osteoarthritis is a chronic bone complication, which is associated with pain in joints. Acetaminophen does not cause any complication in osteoarthritis.
Option 4:Acetaminophen does not increase blood glucose levels and does not worsen the symptoms of type 2 diabetes.

Test Taking Tips: Acetaminophen rarely causes gastrointestinal problems, except in clients who consume alcohol. Recollect the primary result of alcohol consumption to choose the correct answer option.

optionList.content: The client’s platelet count is 80,000 cells/μL blood.
The client’s total cholesterol levels are 120 mg/dL blood.
The client’s fasting blood glucose levels is 80 mg/dL.
The client’s hemoglobin level is 15 g/dL blood.

The client’s hemoglobin level is 15 g/dL blood.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with rheumatoid arthritis is on adalimumab (Humira) therapy. During the follow up visit, the nurse reviews the client’s laboratory reports and concludes that the client has developed adverse effects of medication. Which finding supports the nurse’s conclusion?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Adalimumab (Humira) is an antirheumatic medication. Adalimumab (Humira) may cause thrombocytopenia. A platelet count of less than 150,000 cells/µL blood indicates that the client has thrombocytopenia.
Option 2:Adalimumab (Humira) causes hyperlipidemia. A cholesterol level of 120 mg/dL blood is a normal finding and does not indicate that the client has adverse effects of medication.
Option 3:A fasting blood glucose level of 80 mg/dL indicates that the client has hypoglycemia. However, adalimumab (Humira) neither impairs the pancreatic functioning in the client, nor does it cause hypoglycemia.
Option 4:A hemoglobin level of 15 g/dL is a normal finding and does not indicate that the client has developed adverse effects of adalimumab (Humira).

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with a knee fracture has been prescribed opioid analgesics. The nurse advises the client to cough and to take deep breaths every 2 hours. What is the rationale behind the nurse’s advice?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Opioid analgesics cause respiratory depression, airway obstruction, and increased risk of atelectasis. Atelectasis is collapse of lung tissues, which is commonly caused due to obstruction of the airways. It can be prevented by coughing and deep breathing every 2 hours.
Option 2:Coughing and deep breathing every 2 hours do not prevent nausea and vomiting.
Option 3:A client with orthostatic hypotension has dizziness while changing position. Therefore, nurse would advise the client to change position slowly to prevent orthostatic hypotension.
Option 4:Coughing and deep breathing every 2 hours does not prevent dizziness and drowsiness.

optionList.content: Provide high fiber food to the client
Check for the signs of bruising and bleeding
Give the medication on empty stomach
Check the blood glucose levels of the client
Provide high fiber food to the client
Check for the signs of bruising and bleeding
Give the medication on empty stomach
Check the blood glucose levels of the client

studentQuestionArray.content: A client with myocardial infarction is on aspirin (Acuprin) therapy. Which intervention included in the client’s care plan helps to provide safe and effective treatment?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Aspirin (Acuprin) does not cause constipation. Therefore, the nurse will not recommend high fiber food to the client.
Option 2:Aspirin (Acuprin) reduces platelet aggregation and increases risk of bleeding. Therefore, the nurse should check for signs of bleeding and bruising in the client.
Option 3:Aspirin (Acuprin) is a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medication (NSAID) and causes gastric irritation when taken on an empty stomach.
Option 4:The nurse will not check client’s blood glucose levels, as aspirin (Acuprin) does not cause hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia.

Test Taking Tips: Aspirin can inhibit clumping thereby affecting clot formation. Choose the correct intervention based on this information.

studentQuestionArray.content: While assessing a client with lower limb pain, the nurse finds that the client has bone pain, headache, increased skull size, and decreased visual and auditory acuity. Which medication does the nurse anticipate will be beneficial for the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Allopurinol (Lopurin) decreases uric acid levels and alleviates the symptoms of gout. It is not beneficial for a client who has Paget’s disease.
Option 2:The presence of bone pain, headache, increased skull size, and decreased visual and auditory acuity indicates that the client has Paget’s disease. Alendronate (Fosamax) is a bone resorption inhibitor that inhibits osteoclast activity and alleviates the symptoms of Paget’s disease.
Option 3:Betamethasone (Alphatrex) is a corticosteroid that helps to alleviate symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis, but not Paget’s disease.
Option 4:Isosorbide mononitrate (Isordil) is a nitrate that helps to alleviate symptoms of angina pectoris, but not Paget’s disease.

Test Taking Tips: Paget’s disease is a chronic bone disorder associated with impaired formation of bone and bone remodeling. Knowing the pathophysiology of Paget’s disease will help to identify the appropriate medication for its treatment.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation in a client who is at risk of thrombophlebitis?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Aspirin is an NSAID that inhibits platelet aggregation. A low dose of aspirin is administered to reduce the risk of myocardical infraction, stroke, and thrombophlebitis.
Option 2:Morphine (Avinza) helps to alleviate severe somatic pain, not neuropathic pain. However, morphine (Avinza) does not inhibit platelet aggregation in a client who is at risk of thrombophlebitis.
Option 3:Anticonvulsants such as phenytoin (Phenytek) are prescribed to alleviate neuropathic pain. Phenytoin (Phenytek) does not inhibit platelet aggregation syndrome.
Option 4:Nitroglycerin (Nitrolingual) is a nitrate that helps to alleviate angina, but does not inhibit platelet aggregation.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which fentanyl formulation has rapid onset of action?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Buccal tablet will show its action within 15-30 minutes after administration.
Option 2:Buccal soluble film will show its action after 15 minutes of its administration.
Option 3:Transdermal patch will show its action after 60 minutes of its administration.
Option 4:Oral transmucosal lozenge will show its action rapidly after its administration.

studentQuestionArray.content: A client’s laboratory report is given below: Which parameter indicates the use of methadone (Dolophine) with caution in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:The normal range of hemoglobin is 13.5 to 18 mg/dL in men and 12 to 16 mg/dL in women. A hemoglobin level of 15 g/dL indicates a normal level in the client.
Option 2:The normal range of serum magnesium level is 1.8 to 3 mEq/L. Methadone (Dolophine) should be used with caution in a client with hypomagnesaemia in order to reduce the risk of arrhythmia. However, a serum magnesium level of 2 mEq/L is a normal finding and does not indicate the need to use methadone (Dolophine) with caution.
Option 3:The normal range of potassium is 3.5 to 5 mEq/L. A serum potassium level of 2.4 mEq/L indicates that the client has hypokalemia and a risk of arrhythmia. Therefore, methadone (Dolophine) should be used with caution in the client with hypokalemia.
Option 4:The normal leukocyte count is 5000 to 10,000/MM3. Aleukocyte count of 8,000/MM3 is a normal finding and does not indicate the need to use methadone (Dolophine) with caution in the client.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which behavioral response would the nurse observe while caring for a client with dementia and pain?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Anxiety is a psychological response which is caused by stimulation of the central nervous system.
Option 2:Agitation is a behavioral response which is due to severe pain and anxiety.
Option 3:Exhaustion is a psychological response that is due to fatigue and respiratory depression.
Option 4:A client with schizophrenia will experience hallucinations due to neurochemical imbalance.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which analgesic medication impairs platelet function and increases the risk of bleeding in the client?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Sulphates such as aspirin (Acuprin) impair platelet aggregation and increase the risk of bleeding.
Option 2:Pentazocine (Talwin) is a mixed opioid agonist–antagonist. It does not impair platelet aggregation and does not increase the risk of bleeding.
Option 3:Acetaminophen (Tylenol) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug. It impairs liver functioning, but not platelet aggregation.
Option 4:Buprenorphin (Buprenex) is a partial opioid agonist. It does not impair platelet functioning.

Test Taking Tips: Consider which medication is one of the few measured using the apothecary system.

studentQuestionArray.content: Which condition does the nurse anticipate to find in the client who is on fentanyl (Abstral) therapy?
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Fentanyl (Abstral) is an opioid analgesic, which increases the elimination of potassium and causes hypokalemia.
Option 2:Fentanyl (Abstral) decreases serum potassium levels and increases the absorption of calcium due to which the client has hypercalcemia.
Option 3:Fentanyl (Abstral) reduces the reabsorption of sodium and causes hyponatremia.
Option 4:Fentanyl (Abstral) increases the elimination of magnesium and causes hypomagnesemia.

studentQuestionArray.content: What are the dermatological adverse effects of ibuprofen (Caldolor)? Select all that apply.
studentQuestionArray.textAnalysis:
Rationales

Option 1:Exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis are the skin manifestations of ibuprofen. Ecchymosis is seen with etodolac.
Option 2:Exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis are the skin manifestations of ibuprofen.
Option 3:Exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis are the skin manifestations of ibuprofen.
Option 4:Exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis are the skin manifestations of ibuprofen.
Option 5:Exfoliative dermatitis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis are the skin manifestations of ibuprofen.
